resume:
Priya Ramanathan
Nursing Informatics Intern Candidate | BSN Year 3, University of Michigan School of Nursing
Ann Arbor, MI | priya.ramanathan@umich.example | Expected graduation: May 2027

OBJECTIVE
Third-year nursing student pursuing a summer clinical informatics internship where I can pair bedside experience with my coursework in health data systems. I am especially interested in how electronic health record workflows shape the quality and safety of frontline care, and I want to learn from clinical analysts who sit at the intersection of nursing practice and technology.

CLINICAL EXPERIENCE
Student Nurse, Medical-Surgical Rotation — Michigan Medicine University Hospital (Jan 2026 to Apr 2026)
During my med-surg rotation I cared for assigned patients on a 32-bed adult unit under the supervision of a registered preceptor. My responsibilities included taking vitals, documenting intake and output, performing focused assessments, administering medications under supervision, and charting in the Epic electronic health record. I learned how to use the Medication Administration Record and barcode scanning workflow, and I became comfortable navigating the nursing flowsheets. On a typical shift I documented for four to five patients. I noticed early in the rotation that several of the experienced nurses kept a paper cheat-sheet at the bedside because they found the discharge-checklist screen slow to load, and I started keeping informal notes about which screens caused the most friction. I shared a few of those observations with my preceptor, who encouraged me to write them up.

Student Nurse, Community Health Rotation — Washtenaw County Public Health Clinic (Sep 2025 to Dec 2025)
In my community rotation I supported immunization clinics and basic health screenings. I helped register walk-in patients, verified insurance eligibility at the front desk, and entered demographic information into the clinic intake system. I also shadowed a public-health nurse conducting home visits for new mothers. This rotation gave me my first real exposure to how messy real-world patient data can be: duplicate records, mismatched dates of birth, and addresses that did not match between two systems. I started a small running list of the data-entry errors I saw most often.

PROJECTS AND COURSEWORK
Nursing Informatics (NURS 340), Fall 2025
For my term project in this course, a partner and I analyzed a de-identified sample of discharge documentation timestamps that our instructor provided. The assignment asked us to look for patterns in when discharge instructions were completed relative to the physician discharge order. My partner pulled the data into a spreadsheet and I built the charts. We presented to the class and argued that a meaningful share of discharges were being documented in clusters late in the shift, which we hypothesized contributed to rushed patient education. We received strong feedback from the instructor, though I will be honest that the dataset was small and provided to us pre-cleaned, so I did not have to wrangle the raw extract myself.

Health Data Systems (HINF 210), Winter 2026
This course covered the basics of relational databases, the structure of HL7 messages at a conceptual level, and an introduction to data privacy under HIPAA. I completed lab exercises writing simple SELECT queries against a sample patient database. I would describe my SQL as beginner level: I can filter, join two tables, and group, but I have not written anything against a production system.

Statistics for the Health Sciences (STAT 250), Fall 2024
Introductory statistics including descriptive measures, confidence intervals, and basic hypothesis testing. I used R for the lab portion.

TECHNICAL SKILLS
Electronic Health Records: Epic (clinical-user level, completed the standard student onboarding modules)
Data and analysis: Microsoft Excel including pivot tables, beginner SQL (SELECT, JOIN, GROUP BY), beginner R for coursework, Tableau Public for one class assignment
Clinical: vital signs, focused assessment, medication administration under supervision, documentation in Epic flowsheets
Other: comfortable learning new software quickly, detail oriented with documentation

LEADERSHIP AND ACTIVITIES
Student Nurses Association — member, volunteer at two campus blood drives
Peer tutor for first-year anatomy and physiology, two semesters, roughly three students per week
Volunteer, hospital welcome desk, summer 2024, helped visitors find departments and learned the layout of a large academic medical center

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
I know I am early in my training and that an informatics internship will involve a lot of listening and learning. What I bring is genuine curiosity about why frontline documentation works the way it does, real bedside exposure to the Epic workflows that analysts support, and a habit of writing down the friction I observe rather than just complaining about it. I am hoping to spend a summer close to the people who decide how a screen is built, so that when I graduate and return to the bedside I understand both sides of the chart. I am reliable, I ask a lot of questions, and I am not afraid to say when I do not know something yet.

REFERENCES
Available on request, including my med-surg clinical preceptor and my Nursing Informatics course instructor.
jd: Clinical Informatics Summer Intern (Nursing track) — Regional Health System, Digital & Clinical Systems team. This 10-week paid internship places a nursing student alongside our clinical analysts who configure and optimize the Epic electronic health record for our inpatient units. You will shadow analysts during workflow-optimization sessions, help gather and document frontline pain points from nursing staff, assist with light data pulls and chart reviews under supervision, and contribute to one scoped improvement project that you will present to the Clinical Informatics committee at the end of the summer. We are looking for a current nursing student with recent bedside or clinical rotation experience, exposure to an electronic health record (Epic preferred), comfort with spreadsheets, and clear written communication. SQL exposure is a plus but not required. You must be comfortable interviewing busy clinicians, taking precise notes, and respecting patient-data privacy. This role is supervised throughout; we expect you to ask questions, document carefully, and be honest about the limits of your experience.

history.q: Thanks for coming in. To start, walk me through where you are in your nursing program and why a clinical informatics internship specifically.
Tell me about your med-surg rotation. What were you actually responsible for on a shift?
You mentioned charting in Epic. Which parts of the Epic workflow did you personally use day to day?
You wrote that nurses kept paper cheat-sheets because a screen was slow. Tell me more about what you observed there.
Did you do anything with those observations, or were they just mental notes?
Let's talk about your Nursing Informatics term project. What was the question you were trying to answer?
Walk me through how you got from the raw data to your conclusion about late-shift discharge documentation.
Who did what on that project — what was your specific piece versus your partner's?
How confident are you in that conclusion, given the dataset you had?
Switching gears to skills: how would you describe your SQL level honestly?
This role involves interviewing busy nurses about their workflow pain. Tell me about a time you had to get useful information out of someone who didn't have much time for you.
Stay on that example — once you had gathered those observations from the nurses, what did you actually do with them to make them useful to someone who could act on them?

candidate_last_answer: So during my med-surg rotation, the situation was that the nurses were really slammed and didn't have time to sit down with a student. The task I set myself was to figure out which documentation screens were slowing them down the most. So what I did was, instead of asking for a meeting, I just watched at the bedside and I'd ask one quick question while they were already charting, like 'is this the screen that's slow?' and I kept a little notebook of which screens came up over and over. The discharge checklist came up the most. I tried to make it useful for whoever could act on it.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I tried to make it useful for whoever could act on it

expected-yield: the concrete final step the candidate took to convert her notebook of slow screens into something actionable — did she write it up, who exactly did she give it to (her preceptor by name/role, a charge nurse, an analyst), in what form, and what if anything happened as a result
